text:
Admission Date:  2013-04-29       Discharge Date:  2005-02-19

Date of Birth:   1990-10-01       Sex:  M

Service:  White County Medical Center - South Campus

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  Patient is a 51-year-old
Hispanic man with no known past medical history, who presents
with several days of right facial and neck swelling with
fever, chills, and nausea for several weeks.  He also
complains of polydipsia, polyuria, and a 30 pound weight loss
recently.  He denies any abdominal pain, or vomiting, or
diarrhea.  Patient is very religious and frequently fasts.
He recently fasted for 40 days according to patient in
prayer.  He was recently in [**State 108**] and suspects he drank
something on the plane four days ago that caused all of his
symptoms.  The patient denies any difficulty swallowing.  He
denies shortness of breath, cough, or stridor.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  Negative.

ALLERGIES:  No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS:  Tylenol prn.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  Patient is a minister.  Has six children.
Is married.  He is a devout Diane.  Has frequent fasts.

FAMILY HISTORY:  An aunt and uncle with diabetes.

PHYSICAL EXAM:  Temperature 98.2, pulse 102, blood pressure
128/80, respirations 98% on room air.  Patient is in no acute
distress.  Oropharynx is clear with thrush.  No cervical
lymphadenopathy.  Neck is tender with dramatic swelling, but
supple.  Patient's right cheek with erythema, calor, and
diffuse parotid enlargement.  There is induration.  There are
three areas of "pointing" with thinned skin and fluctuance.
The right buccal and marginal mandibular branch of the facial
nerve are weak with pain and tenderness upon attempted facial
expression.  Patient had good eye closure.  His heart was
regular with no murmurs.  His chest was clear to
auscultation.  His abdomen was soft and nontender.  He had no
hepatosplenomegaly.  Cranial nerves II through XII were
intact.

LABORATORIES UPON ADMISSION:  Showed a white count of 20 with
21 bands, hematocrit 39.8, platelets 165.  Patient's glucose
at Seton Northwest Hospital, where the patient initially
presented with 881, creatinine 1.5, sodium 131, K 4.3.  His
glucose upon admission to the Canton-potsdam Hospital was 512, anion gap of 9.
Urinalysis showed 1000 glucose, no ketones, no nitrites, no
leukocyte esterase.

CAT scan of neck showed a large right parotid mass in the
masseter muscle enlarged with displacement.

Blood and urine cultures were drawn and were negative to
date.  However, blood cultures drawn at Seton Northwest Hospital
showed 4/4 bottles with methicillin-sensitive Staph aureus.

HOSPITAL COURSE BY PROBLEM:
1. Bacterial parotitis:  Patient had initially presented to
Seton Northwest Hospital.  At that time he received IV
clindamycin and Unasyn, and he was transferred to the Canton-potsdam Hospital.
The Ear, Nose, and Throat team saw patient and performed a
debridement in the Emergency Room with incision and drainage.
They packed the wound at that time, and planned to continue
to follow the patient.  Patient had daily dressing changes
with irrigation in the operating room during his admission.

He had a second debridement on day three of hospitalization
as well.  He was followed closely by the Ear, Nose, and
Throat physicians, and will follow up with them as an
outpatient.  The abscess cultures grew MSSA.  The blood
cultures drawn at the Canton-potsdam Hospital are negative to date.  However,
as stated above, blood cultures drawn at Seton Northwest Hospital
showed 4/4 bottles of blood with MSSA.  It was planned to
treat patient with two weeks of IV antibiotics.

Initially upon admission, patient received IV Unasyn for
broad-spectrum oropharyngeal coverage.  However, once culture
results came back, the patient was switched to IV vancomycin
and sensitivities were pending.  Once MSSA was confirmed, the
patient was switched to IV clindamycin.  Patient's white
count trended downwards during his admission and normalized
prior to discharge.  He also became afebrile after spiking
temperatures just after admission to 102.  Patient's dressing
was changed daily by ENT, and he will follow up with them as
an outpatient.  He will be switched to IV oxacillin upon
discharge, and will receive a total of two weeks of IV
antibiotic therapy.  He will have eight days left once he is
discharged.

2. Diabetes:  Patient has new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus
type 2 due to his lack of lipolysis and ketosis upon
admission despite sugars to the 800s.  Patient was
immediately started on aggressive sliding scale insulin as
well as rosiglitazone.  Patient was aggressively hydrated and
had closely monitored sugars.  Initially upon admission the
patient refused to admit that he had the diagnosis of
diabetes, despite reassurance and comforting.  He said he was
a man of God and that no man of God would ever be struck with
this disease.

Despite several hours of attempted reasoning with this
patient, he refused to accept subcutaneous insulin to
continue treating his hyperglycemia.  On evening after
admission, a Psychiatry consult was obtained in order to
assess competency.  The psychiatrist deemed patient competent
to make decisions.  It was at this time it was decided to
transfer the patient to the Intensive Care Unit to begin an
insulin drip and obtain tight control of patient's sugars in
preparation for incision and drainage by ENT.

Patient was transferred to the Intensive Care Unit on 2013-04-29 in the evening and returned to the floor on 02-11.  His sugars were well controlled throughout his
admission, and he was transitioned to an insulin-sliding
scale as directed by the Jackson consult.  Patient's insulin
regimen was 7 units of NPH q.p.m. as well as sliding scale
insulin.  He was also continued on the rosiglitazone 4 mg
p.o. b.i.d.  The Jackson consult service spent large amounts
of time with patient explaining his new diagnosis.  They
initiated multiple teaching sessions and patient began to
accept his diagnosis.

By the time I received him from his ICU course, patient was
in full acceptance of his diagnosis and was eager to be
compliant and be treated.  Patient's initial resistance may
have been due to a slight delirium from his hyperglycemia and
bacteremia, as he was extremely agitated at that time and had
threatened to leave against medical advice.  He was very
cooperative with his diabetes teaching and will continue
getting teaching upon discharge with the Visiting Nurses
Association.  He will check his sugars 4x a day and will
follow up closely with Jackson as scheduled.  He will check
his fingersticks 4x a day.  It is thought that he may be able
to transition off of insulin in the future.

3. Pain control:  Patient had pain with dressing changes and
after incision and drainage.  He was continued on Percocet
prn with good effect.

4. Access:  Patient had a PICC line placed in-house for home
IV antibiotics to complete a two-week course.

5. Disposition:  The patient will be sent home on 06-06
with his wife, and will receive Mrs. Nicole Joseph nursing care for his IV
antibiotics as well as insulin teaching.  He will follow up
with Jackson as well as ENT.  Patient needs a new primary care
physician and [**Name Initial (PRE) **] have sent him to Dr. Rice in [**Company 191**].
Patient will be seen on 03-26 at 2 o'clock.  It is of
note that I will not become this patient's primary care
physician as he was offensive with the use of antisemitic
remarks on the evening of his admission.  Although it is
likely that patient was delirious at this time, I felt
uncomfortable following this patient as an outpatient, and
thus had sent him to Dr. Rice, who is currently accepting
new patients.  Patient was agreeable to this, and intends to
be cooperative with his new physician.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1. Percocet 1-2 tablets p.o. q.4-6h. as needed for pain.
2. Rosiglitazone 4 mg p.o. b.i.d.
3. Multivitamin.
4. Oxacillin 2 grams IV q.6h. for eight days.  Further
antibiotics will be decided upon followup with Ear, Nose, and
Throat.
5. Insulin at a dose of 8 units NPH q.h.s.  His sliding scale
will be stopped Daniel Torres recommendations and may be
restarted in the future.  Jackson did teaching with the One
Touch Ultra glucose monitor, and will prescribe him test
strips and touch lancets prior to discharge.

FOLLOW-UP INSTRUCTIONS:  Patient will have followup at the
Jackson with classes and dieticians.  I will schedule these
for him before he is discharged.



                            [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) 2515**] Thomas, M.D.  95841

Dictated By:Townsend
MEDQUIST36
